title: Cloud Computing for DevOps
description:
## Renting Infrastructure Instead of Owning It

**Cloud computing** means accessing compute, storage, and networking on demand over the internet, instead of buying and racking your own servers. For DevOps, the cloud is the foundation: it turns infrastructure into something you can request, script, and tear down in minutes.

### The major providers

| Provider | Notes |
|---|---|
| **Amazon Web Services (AWS)** | The largest provider; broadest service catalog. |
| **Microsoft Azure** | Strong enterprise and Windows integration. |
| **Google Cloud (GCP)** | Strong in data, Kubernetes (originated GKE), and networking. |
| **DigitalOcean / Hetzner / Linode / Vultr** | Smaller, developer-friendly providers — simpler pricing, fewer services, often cheaper for basic VMs. |

### Regions and availability zones

Providers divide the world into **regions** (geographic areas, e.g. `us-east-1`, `europe-west1`). Each region contains multiple **availability zones (AZs)** — physically separate data centers with independent power and networking. Spreading your workload across AZs survives a single data-center failure; spreading across regions survives a regional outage and reduces latency for distant users.

### The shared responsibility model

Security and operations are **split** between provider and customer:

> The provider is responsible for security **of** the cloud (physical data centers, hardware, the hypervisor). You are responsible for security **in** the cloud (your OS patches, your application code, your IAM permissions, your data).

The exact line moves depending on the service: with a raw VM you patch the OS yourself; with a managed database the provider handles patching for you.

### On-demand and elastic billing

Cloud resources are typically billed **pay-as-you-go** — per second/hour of compute, per GB of storage, per GB of egress traffic. **Elasticity** means you can scale resources up under load and back down when idle, so you pay only for what you use. This is the opposite of buying a fixed fleet of servers sized for peak traffic that sits idle the rest of the time.

### Equivalent core services across providers

| Service type | AWS | Azure | Google Cloud |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Virtual machines (compute)** | EC2 | Virtual Machines | Compute Engine |
| **Object storage** | S3 | Blob Storage | Cloud Storage |

Learning one provider transfers well to the others — the concepts (VMs, object storage, load balancers, managed databases) are nearly universal; only the names and console layouts differ.
